narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46.9% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, LSAT score of 27, Year 1 school decile ranking of 2, and Year 3 decile of 1—all of which are notably lower than the typical student's averages of 3.21 GPA, 36.7 LSAT, 5.71 Year 1 decile, and 5.53 Year 3 decile. Your upper-middle family income bracket aligns closely with the dataset average, while being male, White, and planning full-time enrollment matches the most common profiles among past students. When the model evaluated your application, it weighed these factors to forecast bar passage, and several stood out as the strongest influences pushing toward a failure prediction. The biggest driver by far was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class that year signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. Right behind it, your Year 1 decile played a similar role, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. Your LSAT score came next in influence, with its lower-than-average result adding to the model's caution. Family income had a moderate push in the same direction, followed by your undergraduate GPA, while your race, gender, and full-time status had smaller impacts, all tilting slightly toward the riskier outcome. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, your academic metrics across the board are in the lower range, which collectively outweighed your more typical demographic and enrollment details. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how these patterns showed up in our analysis of thousands of past students. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic record, perhaps by retaking the LSAT to aim higher or excelling in any graduate-level courses if applicable, as those are factors you can realistically influence. Building a stronger trajectory in school deciles through consistent high performance could also shift future predictions in your favor. We encourage you to seek tutoring, study groups, or bar prep resources early, and reapply once you've strengthened these areas—you've got this with the right steps.
